coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots as reductant and stabilizer for one-pot sonochemical synthesis of amorphous carbon-supported silver nanoparticles for electrochemical nonenzymatic H2O2 sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Deli
Zhang, Yuan
Huang, Minhu
Liu, Jie
Wan, Jin
Chu, Haoyu
Chen, Min
coredata.dc:description: We report a one-pot and environment-friendly sonochemical synthesis of amorphous carbon-supported silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) using carbon nanodots derived from biomass, as a combined reducing and capping agent. The as-prepared amorphous carbon-supported silver nanoparticles (Ag/C nanocomposites) exhibited excellent catalytic activity toward electrocatalytic reduction of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Moreover, the Ag/C modified glassy carbon electrode showed a good sensing ability with high sensitivity and low detection limit serving as a nonenzymatic H2O2 sensor. This facile approach to synthesize amorphous carbon-supported Ag NPs not only avoided the use of chemical reductants but also improved the electrocatalytic activity and stability of Ag NPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Ag nanoparticles
Sonochemical synthesis
Nonenzymatic H2O2 sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the modified electrode and electrochemical measurements
Preparation and characterization of Ag/C nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a) and size distribution (b) of the as-prepared CDs.
UV–visible adsorption spectra and corresponding photograph of as-prepared CDs (a) and Ag/C nanocomposites (b).
TEM (a and c) and HRTEM (d) images of as-prepared Ag/C nanocomposite and its size distribution histogram (b).
XRD pattern of as-prepared Ag/C composite.
Raman (a) and (b) FT-IR spectra of as-prepared CDs and Ag/C composite.
TEM images of the Ag/C composites prepared with different Ag+ concentration: 0.02M (a), 0.05M (b), 0.08M (c) and 0.1M (d) under the same CDs concentration (0.1g/L).
CV curves of Ag/C/GCE in pH 7.4, 0.2M air saturated PBS and N2 saturated PBS (scan rate: 50mV/s).
Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of bare GCE, CDs/Ag and Ag/C/GCE electrode in N2-saturated 0.2M PBS at pH 7.4 in the presence of 1.0mM H2O2 (scan rate: 50mV/s).
Cyclic voltammograms of the bare GCE, Ag/C/GCE in 0.1M KCl aqueous solution containing 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1:1) at a scan rate of 50mVs−1.
CVs for Ag/C/GCE in N2-saturated 0.2M PBS at pH 7.4 in the presence of 1.0mM H2O2 at scan rates from 30 to 150mVs−1; the corresponding plot of current vs. the square root of scan rate (inset).

Typical steady-state response of the Ag/C/GCE to successive injection of H2O2 into the stirred N2-saturated 0.2M PBS at pH 7.4 (applied potential: −0.30V). Inset: the magnification and the corresponding calibration plot of steady-state currents against concentrations of H2O2.
Current–time curves for the Ag/C/GCE exposed to AA, UA (1.0mM), glucose (2mM) and H2O2 (1mM) each.
Schematic illustration for the formation mechanism of Ag/C nanocomposites.
A comparison of this work with literature works regarding the performance of the H2O2 assays.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots as reductant and stabilizer for one-pot sonochemical synthesis of amorphous carbon-supported silver nanoparticles for electrochemical nonenzymatic H2O2 sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Ag/C nanocomposite, a nonenzymatic H2O2 sensor, was successfully synthesized through a one-pot sonochemical approach using carbon nanodots derived from biomass, as a combined reducing and capping agent.

We report a one-pot and environment-friendly sonochemical synthesis of amorphous carbon-supported silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) using carbon nanodots derived from biomass, as a combined reducing and capping agent. The as-prepared amorphous carbon-supported silver nanoparticles (Ag/C nanocomposites) exhibited excellent catalytic activity toward electrocatalytic reduction of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Moreover, the Ag/C modified glassy carbon electrode showed a good sensing ability with high sensitivity and low detection limit serving as a nonenzymatic H2O2 sensor. This facile approach to synthesize amorphous carbon-supported Ag NPs not only avoided the use of chemical reductants but also improved the electrocatalytic activity and stability of Ag NPs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) have attracted extensive attention due to their unique shape and size dependent optical, antimicrobial, and catalytic properties [1–3]. The size and size distribution (dispersity), morphology and stability of Ag NPs play pivotal roles in determining their physicochemical properties along with their catalytic activity [4]. Generally, Ag NPs were synthesized by reducing silver ions with reductants such as borohydride, hydrazine, and citrate, followed by surface modification with suitable templates/surfactants to restrict the size of NPs or prevent self-aggregation [5–7]. However, the use of templates/surfactants may introduce heterogeneous impurities and impede catalysis in many catalytic applications [8]. Another problem is that the reducing reagents, such as citric acid, sodium borohydride, or other organic compounds, may exhibit environmental toxicity or biological hazards [9–11]. As the emphasis of science and technology is to move toward environmentally friendly and sustainable resources and processes, much efforts have been made in using naturally produced bio-derived reducing agents to synthesize noble metal NPs.

Earlier reports cite the use of microorganism such as Bacillus koriensis and Bacillus subtilis and fungi such as Aspergillus fumigates and Fusarium oxysporum for the biosynthesis of Ag NPs [12–15]. Recently, several plant extracts such as aloe vera, amla and Capsicum annuum were used for green synthesis of Ag NPs [16–18]. However, these natural sources containing several components generate Ag NPs at much slower rate over a period of 24–48h. In addition, the regional characteristics and low volume output of these plants also limit their wide application. Cellulose is also a candidate for use as a chemical reagent as it can be extracted from any biomass, including waste biomass [19]. Different cellulosic substrates such as porous cellulose fibers [20], nanoporous cellulose gel [21], microbial cellulose filter [22], oxidized cellulose microfibrils [23] were recently used as reductant or supporting medium for the synthesis of Ag NPs. However, the cellulose derivatives used in these reactions remain in the solid, or more-or-less swollen state, due to their insolubility in water and most common organic solvents [24]. Still, size controlled synthesis of Ag NPs and its stability is the major issue to solve. To this regard, it is highly desirable to develop a one-step and convenient method for the rapid synthesis of size-controlled Ag NPs using a green reagent which can be derived from renewable and abundant nature plants.

Photoluminescent carbon nanodots (CDs), a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials, have attracted growing interest due to their promising applications in bioimaging [25], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [26], electrocatalysis [27] and photocatalysis [28]. More recently, plenty of researchers focus attention on using natural biomass such as grass [29], soybean [27], willow bark [30] and orange juice [31] to synthesize carbon nanodots (CDs). The most important feature of this green synthetic route is that the carbonization, surface functionalization and element doping will occur simultaneously during the hydrothermal treatment. On the other hand, these surface-functionalized CDs have been proved to be effective fluorescent sensing platforms for the ultrasensitive detection of metal ions (Ag(I) [32], Cu(II) [29] and Hg(II) [33]), which is on the basis of fluorescent quenching of CDs caused by the interaction or chelation between metal ions and the functional groups on the surface of CDs. Inspired by these phenomena, researchers have successfully synthesized gold nanoparticles using CDs as reductant and capping agent upon heating [34] or under UV irradiation [30]. In our previous report, Ag/C nanocomposites were obtained using CDs prepared from biomass as reductant, in the presence of polyvinyl pyrrolidone as capping agent, through a hydrothermal process [35].

In this paper, for the first time, we present a green sonochemical synthetic route toward the rapid synthesis and stabilization of Ag NPs using CDs derived from natural biomass, as a combined reducing and capping reagent. Size-controlled amorphous carbon supported Ag NPs (Ag/C) nanocomposites could be obtained by appropriately changing the molar ratio of silver ions and CDs. Importantly, the as-synthesized Ag/C nanocomposites exhibited excellent catalytic activity for electrocatalytic hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) reduction. The as-fabricated Ag/C modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as a nonenzymatic H2O2 sensor showed high sensitivity and low detection limit. This facile approach to synthesize amorphous carbon-supported Ag NPs not only avoided the use of chemical reductants but also improved the catalytic activity and stability of Ag NPs.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30wt%) and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chem. Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3) were purchased from Aldrich Chemical Comp. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The Zoysiagrass was obtained from the ground of Jiangsu University and washed by water for further use. The water used was double-distilled after a deionized exchange.

CDs were prepared according to the method reported previously [29], and the details are described as follows. Firstly, Zoysiagrass (4g) was added into 20mL of water. Then the mixture was transferred into a 50mL Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 180°C for 3h. A brown solution containing CDs was collected through centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 10min. The CDs solution thus obtained was diluted to fixed volume (25mL) for further use (0.1g/L).

In a typical preparation, 10mL of AgNO3 solution with different concentration (0.02M, 0.05M, 0.08M and 0.1M) was slowly added to 10mL (0.5mg/L) as-prepared CDs solution with vigorous stirring. After stirring for about 15min, the mixture was then sealed and sonicated (Ultrasonic Cleaner AS2060B, 40KHz, 60W) for 1h at constant 313K. The dark green precipitates were collected by centrifugation at 8000rpm, and then washed with deionized water and ethanol for 3 times, respectively. Finally, the sample was dispersed in ethanol for further characterization.

For comparison, unsupported silver nanoparticles were prepared according to Turkevich’s method [36]: 20mL of AgNO3 aqueous solution (0.001M) was put into a round-bottom flask and heated to boiling. Then, 0.5mL of aqueous solution of sodium citrate (1% by weight) was added under magnetic stirring. After reaction for 40min, a grayish yellow suspension was obtained.

Purity and crystallization of the samples were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction on a D/max-γA X-ray spectrometer (Rigaku, Japan) at 40kV and 200mA with monochromatic Cu Kα (λ =1.5418Å) radiation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a TECNAI12 TEM instrument (Philips) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The sample for TEM characterization was prepared by placing a drop of the dispersion on a carbon-coated copper grid and dried at room temperature. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy measurements were performed on a UV-2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer. Raman scattering was recorded by a Thermo Electron Corporation DXR Raman spectrometer (USA) using a 532nm laser source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the modified electrode and electrochemical measurements
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5mg of Ag/C composite and 10μL of Nafion solution (5wt%) were dispersed in 1mL water–isopropanol mixed solvent (3:1 v/v) by at least 30min sonication to form a homogeneous catalyst solution. The GCE was carefully polished in 0.3 and 0.05nm alumina slurry, respectively, and washed with ultrapure water followed by sonication in water for 10min. Then 10μL of the catalyst solution was dropped onto the surface of the cleaned GCE and left to dry at room temperature to get Ag/C-modified GCE, referred to as Ag/C/GCE. Amperometric and cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were carried out by a CHI 650D electrochemical analyzer (CH Instruments, Inc., Shanghai), in a home-made three-electrode electrochemical cell consisting of a twisted platinum wire as an auxiliary electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference electrode and a modified-GCE (0.07cm2) as a working electrode. All experiments were carried out at the ambient temperature. Prior to the experiment, the electrolyte consisting of a solution of 0.2M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4), was purged with high-purity nitrogen for at least 30min and a nitrogen atmosphere was maintained over the solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Ag/C nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The structure and morphology of the initial CDs for preparing Ag/C nanocomposites were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, it can be clearly seen that the as-prepared CDs are uniform and monodisperse. It is found that their diameters are mainly distributed in the range of 3–5nm (Fig. 1b).

Fig. 2 shows the UV–visible adsorption spectra of as-synthesized CDs and Ag/C nanocomposites and corresponding photographs. It can be seen from the UV–visible spectra of CDs (curve a in Fig. 2) that there is a strong absorption peak at about 260nm, which is consistent with the literature [14]. As for Ag/C composites, the peak at 448nm was attributed to the local surface plasmon resonances of Ag nanoparticles. The freshly prepared CDs solution is transparent light yellow. After ultrasonic irradiation in the presence of silver ions (0.05M), the suspension color changed from light yellow to dark green, which also demonstrates the formation of Ag NPs. In addition, the dark green suspension of the Ag/C nanocomposites remained stable for several months or even longer because of the stabilization of CDs.

In order to study the microstructure of the Ag/C composite in more detail, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) observations were carried out. Fig. 3 a presents the TEM image of the amorphous carbon-supported Ag NPs nanohybrids along with the corresponding particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 3b). It could be distinctly seen that Ag NPs were well dispersed and attached to the amorphous carbon. Moreover, the Ag NPs show a narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 11nm. At high magnification TEM image (Fig. 3c), it can be found that the Ag NPs are approximately spherical in shape and anchored on the amorphous carbon which consists of many irregular cross-linked substructures. The HRTEM image of one nanoparticle gives us further insight into the microstructure and crystallinity of the as-prepared Ag/C composite (Fig. 3d). The interplanar spacings of 2.4Å and 2.0Å were clearly observed, which correspond to (111) and (200) crystallographic planes of Ag, respectively, in good agreement with the standard card JCPDS No. 04-0783.

The XRD pattern of as-prepared Ag/C composites is shown in Fig. 4 , four diffraction peaks with 2θ values of 38.5°, 44.6°, 64.8°, 77.7° can be observed in the pattern, corresponding to diffractions from the (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes of face-centered-cubic (fcc) phase which indicates that the Ag particles are well crystallized. And the amorphous nature of the carbon is demonstrated by the wide diffraction peak around 2θ value of 27°. This result is consistent with the above-mentioned TEM and HRTEM observations.

Fig. 5 a shows the Raman spectra of the as-prepared CDs and Ag/C composite. The spectrum of CDs did not show any obvious Raman signal due to the disturbance of its strong fluorescence background. However, in the Raman spectrum of the Ag/C composite, two peaks were observed at 1360 and 1583cm−1, which are the typical D-band and G-band of conventional carbon nanomaterials, respectively. The similar broad D-band peak, which frequently appeared in the Raman characterization ascribed to the defects in the carbon-based materials, is related to structural disorder [8], indicating the low degree of crystallization of the amorphous carbon. The spectral observations also imply that the strong fluorescence of CDs was partly quenched attributed to the electron transfer from CDs to Ag nanoparticles [34], indicating the successful synthesis of the Ag/C composites.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was further used to discern the structure and component of the CDs and the Ag/C composite. As shown in Fig. 5a, stretching vibrations of OH at 3453cm−1, vibrations of COH at 1068cm−1, stretching vibration of CO at 1641cm−1, and vibrations of COOH at 1398cm−1 were observed in the spectrum of CDs [27,37]. In the FT-IR spectrum of Ag/C, almost all the IR absorption peaks from CDs still remained. However, the intensity of the vibrations of CO at 1641cm−1 and OH at 3453cm−1 greatly receded, suggesting that CO, HO bond partly breaking during the formation of Ag NPs [38]. These FT-IR observations indicate that the oxygenated functional groups on the surface of CDs played important roles in the formation of silver nanoparticles [37].

The influence of the molar ratio of Ag+ to CDs on the synthesis of the Ag/C composites was investigated. Fig. 6 shows the TEM images of the Ag/C composites prepared with different ratios of Ag+ to CDs under the same CDs concentration. As can be seen Fig. 6(a), when less Ag+ ions (0.02M) were added, small Ag particles with good dispersity and some Ag nanoclusters (1–2nm in size) attached on the amorphous carbon were obtained. As the concentration of Ag+ ions increased, a mass of irregular bigger Ag NPs and their aggregates appeared. These observations indicated that excess CDs played an important role in stabilizing small Ag NPs. Size-controlled synthesis of Ag NPs could be achieved by simply adjusting the molar ratio of Ag+ to CDs. In addition, for comparison purpose, 10mL distilled water instead of 10mL CDs solution was used in the sonochemical synthetic process with other reaction conditions unchanged. It was found that transparent solution was formed after ultrasonic irradiation. Based on the above discussions, it could be concluded that CDs used in our reaction system was a combined reducing and capping reagent. The samples prepared with 0.05M Ag+ ions were used for following catalytic activity tests.

On the basis of previous reports [35,39–42], a possible mechanism was proposed for the preparation of the Ag/C composites. Firstly, Ag+ ions were adsorbed by the oxygenous groups on the surface of CDs through the interaction or chelation, facilitating the aggregation of the CDs. Ultrasonic treatment of the reaction solution generated highly reactive H
and OH
radicals. Meanwhile, these OH
radicals further reacted with the organic groups (RH) of the CDs, generating secondary radicals (R
) which have strong reducing power. Then the adsorbed Ag+ were reduced to Ag0 by these H
and R
radicals. At the same time, the CDs were oxidized. The stable structure of the CDs was damaged, leading to the formation of the amorphous carbon made up of many polymer chains. By prolonging the sonication time, the amorphous carbon-supported small Ag NPs could be obtained. Besides, the excess oxygenous groups provide stability for Ag NPs and prevent further growth of small particles. The schematic illustration of the synthetic approach is shown in Scheme 1 .

H2O2 is a chemical used widely in the food, pharmaceutical, paper, and chemical industries. It is also a by-product of the reaction catalyzed by a large number of oxidase enzymes. The precise and rapid detection of H2O2 is of practical significance for both biochemical and environmental applications. In comparison with the conventional techniques for H2O2 determination, electrochemistry based on a biosensor show the advantages of low cost, easy preparation, rapid detection, low consumption, high selectivity and sensitivity. The promising application as an enzyme-free biosensor for H2O2 was selected as a preliminary test of the electrocatalytic behaviors of as-prepared Ag/C nanocomposites.

To testify the electrocatalytic activity of as-prepared Ag/C nanocomposites, we designed an enzymeless H2O2 sensor by direct deposition of the Ag/C composites on a bare GCE surface. The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) activity of Ag/C/GCE is shown in Fig. 7 . An obvious reduction peak centered at about −0.7V can be clearly seen in air saturated 0.2M pH 7.4 PBS (the dotted curve in Fig. 7), while no obvious reduction peak can be observed in N2 saturated 0.2M pH 7.4 PBS (the solid curve in Fig. 7), indicating an electroreduction of dissolved oxygen on Ag/C/GCE which may cause interference on the current response of H2O2 detection. Therefore, removing oxygen is essential in order to obtain accurate results.

Fig. 8 shows the electrocatalytic responses of bare GCE, CDs modified GCE (CDs/GCE), unsupported Ag NPs modified GCE (Ag/GCE), Ag/C nanocomposites modified GCE (Ag/C/GCE) toward the reduction of H2O2 in N2 saturated 0.2M PBS at pH 7.4 in the presence of 1mM H2O2. It can be obviously seen that the responses of both the bare GCE and CDs/GCE toward the reduction of H2O2 are quite weak. It can be found that Ag/GCE exhibits an obvious catalytic current peak about 47.8μA in intensity at −0.56V vs. SCE. In contrast, the Ag/C/GCE exhibits an enhanced remarkable catalytic current peak about 83μA in intensity at −0.53V vs. SCE, indicating the higher catalytic activity of Ag/C for electrochemical reduction of H2O2. It is also important to notice that the Ag/C/GCE exhibits no electrochemical response in the absence of H2O2. These results suggest that Ag/C has a good electrocatalytic activity toward the H2O2 reduction and the Ag NPs anchored on the amorphous carbon play an important role as the main electroactive materials. Meanwhile, it exhibits a 90mV positive shift of the peak potential compared to hydrothermal synthesis of PQ11-AgNPs decorated GCE [43], respectively.

The electrochemical properties of the Ag/C nancomposite was then further investigated with an electrochemical active probe, [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and compared against that of a bare electrode. As shown in Fig. 9 , a couple of typical redox peaks appeared at the bare GCE. However, a decrease of the redox current peaks was observed at the Ag/C/GCE, which can be explained by the fact that the negatively charged oxygen containing moieties of the CDs support repels the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− anions from the electrode surface and thus results in an enhanced electron-transfer resistance [44,45]. In the previous study of the carbon nanodots applied in electrocatalytic oxygen reduction, Zhu et al. also found the similar phenomena [27]. And this also indicates that the Ag/C nanocomposites have been successfully assembled onto the GCE electrode.

In addition, the effect of scan rate on the sensor was also examined. As can be observed from Fig. 10 , the peak current increased accordingly with the scan rates in the range of 30–150mVs−1. The peak currents increase linearly with the square root of the scan rate, indicating a diffusion-controlled processes.

From these results, the well-dispersed Ag NPs and the amorphous carbon support show a synergistic effect on the superior electrocatalytic activity. The mechanism for H2O2 electro-reduction can be expressed as follows [46–48]:
(1)
H
2
O
2
+
e
-
→
OH
ad
+
OH
-
(2)
OH
ad
+
e
-
→
OH
-
(3)
2
OH
-
+ 2
H
+
→ 2
H
2
O

The formation of OHad (i.e., chemisorbed OH) is a key step controlling the reaction rate in the reduction of H2O2. Ag atoms could function as the sites for chemisorption of OHad and subsequent reduction to OH−. For as-prepared Ag/C composite modified electrode, the well-dispersed Ag NPs with high number density and the surfactant-free surface attributed to the green synthetic approach could make the main contributions to the observed excellent catalytic activity [37]. In addition, the amorphous carbon support facilitated the charge transfer between the active layer and the glassy carbon (GC) electrode [49,50].

In order to further evaluate the sensing ability as a nonenzymatic sensor toward the detection of H2O2, the amperometric response to H2O2 on the as-prepared Ag/C nanocomposites was recorded at −0.3V in Fig. 11 . To avoid or decrease the interference of other electro-active species and minimize the background current, an applied potential of −0.3V was used for the amperometric determination of H2O2 [51]. It is noted that an obvious steady-state current response can be observed when H2O2 concentration reaches 4μM in the figure below (inset of Fig. 11). And the current increases stepwise with successive additions of H2O2. The current response displays a good liner behavior in a concentration range from 4×10−3 to 25.5mM (as shown in the inset of Fig. 11) with a linear regression equation of I (μA)=−8.52c (mM) −1.13 (R =0.997). The detection limit is estimated to be 1.3μM at the signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (see Fig. 12 ).

The detailed comparison of our present work with others regarding the performance of H2O2 assays is presented in Table 1 . With a wider linear response range, a relatively lower limit of detection and fast current response for H2O2, as-prepared Ag/C nanocomposites can even be compared advantageously with respect to most Ag-based nonenzymatic H2O2 sensors [43–45,52–57]. More importantly, the preparation of this sensor is much cheaper and easier as compared with that of enzyme-based or protein-based H2O2 sensors [58].

For practical applications, we also investigate the selectivity of the Ag/C composite modified electrode for H2O2 detection. The influence from common interference species such as ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA) and glucose (Glu) was investigated by using their relevant physiological levels [59]. It is seen that the Ag/C/GCE shows the anti-interference advantage by comparing the amperometric responses of four relevant electroactive species (UA, AA, 1.0mM, and glucose, 2.0mM, respectively) and H2O2 (1.0mM) at a working potential of −0.3V as shown in Fig. 10. The good ability of anti-interference is largely attributed to the low working potential of −0.3V used in the determination of H2O2 [60]. In addition, the as-prepared Ag/C modified GCE shows good repeatability with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.7% for five successive detections at 1.0mM H2O2. Also, it exhibits an acceptable reproducibility with a RSD of 4.8% for five individual electrodes. Additionally, the current response retained 91% of its initial value after a storage period of 3weeks, indicating the good long-term stability of as-prepared Ag/C composites.

In summary, the work shows a one-pot green synthesis of amorphous carbon supported Ag NPs directly from an aqueous AgNO3 and carbon nanodots solution in the absence of other reducing agents and capping agents. The size of Ag NPs anchored on the amorphous carbon can be controlled by simply changing the molar ratio of AgNO3 and carbon nanodots. Importantly, this facile approach to synthesize amorphous carbon-supported Ag NPs not only avoided the use of chemical reductants but also improved the electrocatalytic activity and stability of Ag NPs toward electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2. Moreover, the as-prepared Ag/C nanocomposites have a good sensing ability with high sensitivity and low detection limit serving as a nonenzymatic H2O2 sensor. This method opens up a facile route to synthesize other noble metal (e.g., Au, Pd, etc.) NPs using CDs derived from biomass as a combined reducing and capping agent for electrocatalytic applications.
There is no conflict of interest in this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the financial supports of Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research Funds (1202040C), College Natural Science Research Program of Jiangsu Province (13KJB610003), Zhenjiang Industry Supporting Plan (GY2013023) and Zhenjiang Social Developing Plan (SH2013002) and Research Foundation for Talented Scholars of Jiangsu University (11JDG149 and 10JDG133).
